title: How Solar Panels Work
summary: Understanding photovoltaic technology and how sunlight becomes electricity for your home.
content:
Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity through the photovoltaic effect. Here's how it works:

**1. Sunlight Hits the Panels**
Solar panels are made of silicon cells that absorb photons (light particles) from the sun.

**2. Electrons Are Released**
When photons hit the silicon, they knock electrons loose from their atoms, creating an electrical current.

**3. DC to AC Conversion**
The solar panels produce direct current (DC) electricity. An inverter converts this to alternating current (AC), which is what your home uses.

**4. Powering Your Home**
The AC electricity flows through your electrical panel and powers your home. Any excess can go back to the grid.

**5. Net Metering**
Many utilities offer net metering, where excess solar energy you send to the grid earns you credits on your bill.

**Key Facts:**
• Modern panels are 20-22% efficient
• Panels produce power even on cloudy days (at reduced output)
• Most residential systems are 5-12 kW
• Panels last 25-30+ years with minimal maintenance

title: Understanding Your Electric Bill
summary: Learn what each charge on your utility bill means and how to identify areas for savings.
content:
Your electric bill may seem confusing, but every charge has a purpose. Here's a breakdown:

**Base/Service Charge**
A fixed monthly fee for being connected to the grid, regardless of how much electricity you use. Typically $8-$20/month.

**Energy Usage Charges**
The main portion of your bill. You're charged per kilowatt-hour (kWh) used. Many utilities use tiered pricing:
• Tier 1: Lowest rate for baseline usage
• Tier 2: Higher rate for above-baseline usage
• Tier 3: Highest rate for heavy usage

**Delivery/Transmission Charges**
Fees for delivering electricity from the power plant to your home through the grid infrastructure.

**Taxes & Fees**
Government taxes and various regulatory fees that fund public programs, renewable energy initiatives, and infrastructure maintenance.

**Reading Your Meter**
Your kWh usage is measured by your electric meter. Digital smart meters report usage automatically and can track time-of-use patterns.

**Tips for Savings:**
• Stay within Tier 1 usage when possible
• Consider a time-of-use plan if you can shift usage to off-peak hours
• Compare your usage to previous months to spot anomalies

title: Solar Incentives & Tax Credits
summary: Federal and state incentives that can reduce the cost of going solar by 30% or more.
content:
Going solar is more affordable than ever thanks to generous government incentives:

**Federal Investment Tax Credit (ITC)**
The biggest incentive available. As of 2024:
• 30% tax credit on the total cost of your solar system
• Available through 2032
• Steps down to 26% in 2033, 22% in 2034
• Must own the system (not lease) to claim
• Can be carried forward if you don't owe enough taxes

**State Incentives**
Many states offer additional benefits:
• State tax credits (varies by state)
• Solar Renewable Energy Credits (SRECs)
• Property tax exemptions for solar installations
• Sales tax exemptions on equipment

**Net Metering**
Most states require utilities to credit you for excess solar energy sent to the grid at the retail electricity rate.

**Utility Rebates**
Some utilities offer direct rebates for installing solar, typically $0.25-$1.00 per watt.

**Example Savings:**
For a $24,000 system:
• Federal ITC (30%): -$7,200
• State credit: -$1,000 to -$3,000
• Net cost: $13,800 - $15,800

**Important:** Consult a tax professional to maximize your incentives.

title: Common Solar Myths Debunked
summary: Separating fact from fiction about residential solar energy.
content:
Let's clear up some common misconceptions about solar energy:

**Myth: Solar panels don't work in cloudy weather**
Fact: Panels still produce 25-45% of their rated output on cloudy days. Germany, one of the cloudiest countries in Europe, is a world leader in solar.

**Myth: Solar panels are too expensive**
Fact: Solar costs have dropped 70% since 2010. With the 30% federal tax credit and financing options, many homeowners pay $0 upfront.

**Myth: Solar panels damage your roof**
Fact: When properly installed, solar panels actually protect the roof beneath them from weather. Reputable installers use weatherproof mounting systems.

**Myth: Solar panels require constant maintenance**
Fact: Solar panels have no moving parts and require minimal maintenance. An occasional rinse with water and annual inspection is typically sufficient.

**Myth: I need a south-facing roof**
Fact: While south-facing is optimal, east and west-facing roofs still produce 80-90% of a south-facing roof's output.

**Myth: Solar panels decrease home value**
Fact: Studies show solar panels increase home value by 3-4% on average. Homes with solar sell faster too.

**Myth: I should wait for better technology**
Fact: Current panels are highly efficient and last 25+ years. Waiting means missing years of savings and incentives that may decrease.

title: How to Choose a Solar Installer
summary: What to look for when selecting a solar installation company.
content:
Choosing the right solar installer is crucial. Here's what to evaluate:

**Credentials**
• NABCEP certification (North American Board of Certified Energy Practitioners)
• State contractor's license
• Proper insurance and bonding
• Years in business (look for 5+)

**Reputation**
• Online reviews (Google, BBB, SolarReviews)
• Customer references
• Portfolio of completed installations
• Better Business Bureau rating

**Proposals & Quotes**
• Get at least 3 quotes
• Compare system size, panel brand, and warranty terms
• Ask about production guarantees
• Understand what's included (permits, interconnection, monitoring)

**Equipment Quality**
• Tier 1 solar panels (LG, SunPower, REC, Panasonic)
• Reputable inverter brands (Enphase, SolarEdge)
• 25-year panel warranty minimum
• 10-year workmanship warranty

**Financing Transparency**
• Clear pricing with no hidden fees
• Multiple financing options offered
• Help with tax credit and incentive paperwork

**Red Flags**
• High-pressure sales tactics
• No written contract or warranty
• Significantly lower price than competitors
• No local office or references
• Demands full payment upfront

title: Solar Battery Storage Explained
summary: How home batteries work with solar panels for energy independence.
content:
Battery storage lets you save excess solar energy for later use:

**How It Works**
During the day, excess solar energy charges the battery instead of going to the grid. At night or during outages, the battery powers your home.

**Popular Options**
• Tesla Powerwall: 13.5 kWh, ~$11,500 installed
• Enphase IQ Battery: 3.36-10.08 kWh, modular design
• LG RESU: 9.6-16 kWh, compact size
• Generac PWRcell: 9-18 kWh, flexible capacity

**Benefits**
• Backup power during outages
• Maximize self-consumption of solar energy
• Avoid peak electricity rates (TOU plans)
• Potential to go fully off-grid
• Eligible for the 30% federal tax credit

**Considerations**
• Adds $10,000-$20,000 to your solar investment
• Batteries last 10-15 years
• May not be cost-effective if you have good net metering
• Best for areas with frequent outages or TOU rates

**Is It Worth It?**
Batteries make the most financial sense if:
• Your utility has time-of-use rates
• Net metering rates are below retail
• You experience frequent power outages
• You want energy independence
